title: Workbook — The Virtues
html:
Student Workbook

To use after class or in a group setting. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. How does the Catechism define a virtue? Write the definition in your own words.
  2. What are the four cardinal virtues and why are they called that?
  3. What does it mean that prudence is "the charioteer of the virtues"?
  4. Explain the difference between an acquired virtue and an infused virtue. Give an example of each.
  5. What are the three theological virtues? Why can they not be acquired through human effort?
  6. Name at least 5 gifts of the Holy Spirit and 5 fruits of the Holy Spirit.

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no "right" answers in the personal questions.

1. Of the four cardinal virtues (prudence, justice, fortitude, temperance), which do you most need to cultivate right now? Why?

Be specific: think of a situation in your life where that virtue would help you act better.

2. Thomas More said before dying: "I die the King's good servant, but God's first." Is there something in your life where you have to choose between pleasing others and being faithful to what you believe?

It could be something small (staying silent when you should speak, or following the crowd when you know it's wrong).

3. Charity is the greatest of all the virtues (1 Cor 13:13). Is there someone you find hard to treat with charity?

Write their name (or initial) and think of a small act of charity you can do for that person this week.

4. St. Paul says: "Whatever is true, whatever is honorable, whatever is just, whatever is pure ... think about these things" (Phil 4:8). What kind of content do you consume every day? (Social media? Music? Shows?)

Do those things help you grow in virtues or distract you from them? Be honest.

5. From the twelve fruits of the Holy Spirit, choose ONE you want to see grow in you. Write why you chose it and what you will do this week to cultivate it.

Examples: peace — dedicate 5 minutes a day to silence without screens. Patience — don't complain when something doesn't go as expected.

Practical Activities

A. The Tree of Virtues

Draw a large tree on a sheet of paper. In the roots write: "Grace of God" and "Sacraments." On the trunk write: "The Four Cardinal Virtues." On the main branches write: "Faith," "Hope," and "Charity." On the smaller branches write the fruits of the Spirit you already recognize in your life. On the empty branches, write the fruits you don't yet see but would like to see bloom. At the foot of the tree write: "He who abides in me, and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit" (Jn 15:5).

B. Virtue Check-Up

Do a brief examination of conscience focused on the virtues. Rate each cardinal virtue in your life NOW from 1 (very weak) to 5 (very strong):

Virtue1-5One action to improve it
Prudence (I discern before acting)______
Justice (I give each their due)______
Fortitude (I remain firm in the good)______
Temperance (I moderate my desires)______

Choose the virtue with the lowest score. That is your priority growth area this month.

C. Dialogue with Thomas More

Imagine you can speak with St. Thomas More from his cell in the Tower of London. You ask him: "How did you manage to stay firm when everyone asked you to yield?" Write his imaginary answer (8-10 lines) based on what you know of him and the virtues he lived.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Memory Verse

"So faith, hope, love abide, these three; but the greatest of these is love."

— 1 Corinthians 13:13

Prayer for This Week

Come, Holy Spirit,
fill the hearts of your faithful
and kindle in them the fire of your love.
Grant me prudence to discern your will,
justice to give each what they deserve,
fortitude not to give up in the face of difficulties,
and temperance to use well the gifts you have given me.
Increase in me faith, hope, and charity,
and make the fruits of your Spirit
visible in my life.
Amen.

Print Workbook
